extracted_rules.resource: Right to reside in sacred precincts
proof: [§106] Secondly, when he founded Seleucea on the river Tigris and brought to it Babylonian colonists he spared the wall of Babylon as well as the sanctuary of Bel, near which he permitted the Chaldeans to live.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text notes that Seleucus permitted the Chaldeans (the priestly/learned class of Babylon) to maintain their residence specifically near their sanctuary, even while relocating other populations to the new city of Seleucea.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies specifically to the Chaldean class within the Mesopotamian region under Hellenistic rule.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to invoke Mercy in legal/civic life
proof: [§110] In the Athenian market-place among the objects not generally known is an altar to Mercy, of all divinities the most useful in the life of mortals and in the vicissitudes of fortune, but honored by the Athenians alone among the Greeks.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Pausanias identifies the cult of Mercy (Eleos) as a unique Athenian civic institution. This altar provided a concrete mechanism for individuals (often suppliants or those in legal distress) to seek protection/relief, which Pausanias identifies as a specific 'utility' in Athenian life.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This resource is available to the general population of the city-state of Athens.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to political legitimacy and office
proof: [§110] While Theseus was thus kept in bonds, the sons of Tyndareus marched against Aphidna, captured it and restored Menestheus to the kingdom. Now Menestheus took no account of the children of Theseus, who had secretly withdrawn to Elephenor in Euboea.
extracted_rules.reasoning: In the context of Athenian political transitions, the text shows that a new ruler (Menestheus) systematically ignores or displaces the offspring of the previous ruler to consolidate power, forcing them into exile.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies only to the immediate family of a specific deposed leader.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to enter sacred spaces
proof: [§121] Into this neither stranger nor priest may enter, until they bury Apis.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Pausanias describes a specific Egyptian religious restriction regarding the sanctuary of Serapis at Memphis, where 'strangers' (non-locals/foreigners) are barred from entry during certain periods (until the burial of Apis).
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule applies specifically to the Egyptian sanctuary of Serapis at Memphis during a particular ritual timeframe, affecting a specific subset of visitors.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to enter sacred spaces
proof: [§121] Into this neither stranger nor priest may enter, until they bury Apis.
extracted_rules.reasoning: In the context of the Egyptian sanctuary at Memphis, even the 'priests' (an occupational group) are barred from entering the site during the period preceding the burial of Apis.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This is a very specific professional/ritual restriction applied to a small group of cult officials at a single location.

extracted_rules.resource: Freedom of movement in public
proof: [§128] When he came to the temple of the Delphinian, wearing a tunic that reached to his feet and with his hair neatly plaited, those who were building the roof mockingly inquired what a marriageable virgin was doing wandering about by herself.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The mockery directed at Theseus (who was mistaken for a young woman due to his attire) indicates a social norm in Athens where 'marriageable virgins' were expected not to be seen 'wandering about' alone in public spaces such as temple construction sites.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This reflects a general gendered social expectation for a significant portion of the female population in the city state.

extracted_rules.resource: Protection from execution without trial
proof: [§140] When Sulla returned to Attica he imprisoned in the Cerameicus the Athenians who had opposed him, and one chosen by lot out of every ten he ordered to be led to execution.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes the historical practice of decimation applied by Sulla to the 'lower orders' and 'turbulent' Athenians who opposed him. This shows that in the context of Roman occupation/conflict, a specific segment of the Athenian population lost their right to life and legal process based on their political alignment and socioeconomic status.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to a significant minority of the population—those who participated in a specific political faction (the 'lower orders' and 'turbulent' ones) during a time of war.

extracted_rules.resource: Protection from summary execution
proof: [§141] Although Sulla's treatment of the Athenian people was so savage as to be unworthy of a Roman, I do not think that this was the cause of his calamity, but rather the vengeance of the suppliants' Protector, for he had dragged Aristion from the sanctuary of Athena, where he had taken refuge, and killed him.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Pausanias indicates that there was a standard rule/norm protecting people who took refuge in a sanctuary. Sulla's violation of this (dragging Aristion from the sanctuary) is noted specifically because it breached a settled customary rule concerning the safety of suppliants.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to a specific subset of people: those in immediate legal or physical danger who seek refuge in sacred spaces.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to choose political alliance
proof: [§139] He induced the Athenians to join Mithridates rather than the Romans, although he did not induce all, but only the lower orders, and only the turbulent among them. The respectable Athenians fled to the Romans of their own accord.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text distinguishes between the 'lower orders' and the 'respectable Athenians' (the wealthy/elite), showing that the latter had the mobility and political autonomy to choose their own alliance (fleeing to the Romans) while the former were mobilized by an envoy.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This describes a broad divide in the Athenian citizen body based on wealth and social standing.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to trial for homicide
proof: [§142] In it there is a spring, by which they say that Poseidon's son Halirrhothius deflowered Alcippe the daughter of Ares, who killed the ravisher and was the first to be put on his trial for the shedding of blood.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Pausanias identifies the trial of Ares as the foundational legal precedent for homicide trials in Athens. This indicates a societal rule where 'shedding of blood' must be adjudicated in a court rather than settled through extrajudicial feuding.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This describes the fundamental legal process for any person involved in a homicide within the community.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to private land allotments
proof: [§142] Each man keeps many mares, since the land is not divided into private allotments, nor does it bear any thing except wild trees, as the people are nomads.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Pausanias describes the Sauromatae society as lacking private land ownership, contrasting their nomadic customary rules regarding resources with the settled, divided land of the Greeks.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to an entire ethnic/social group (the Sauromatae) and defines their relationship to their primary resource, land.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to inherit throne over elder siblings
proof: [§150] When Theseus was about to marry Phaedra, not wishing, should he have children, Hippolytus either to be their subject or to be king in their stead, sent him to Pittheus to be brought up and to be the future king of Troezen.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a rule of political succession where children from a current royal marriage (Phaedra's) would have a claim to the throne that conflicts with a son from a previous union (Hippolytus). To avoid this conflict of political power, the elder son is exported to a different kingdom.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule specifically concerns royal succession and the hierarchy of heirs within a ruling family.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to possess and transmit sacred names
proof: [§150] There is also a sanctuary of Earth, Nurse of Youth, and of Demeter Chloe (Green). You can learn all about their names by conversing with the priests.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Pausanias indicates that specific sacred information (the names of the deities) is held by the priests, who have the authority to grant or withhold this knowledge from visitors.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: The rule applies to a specific professional group within the sanctuary system.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to public memorialization
proof: [§159] As a recompense, when the tyranny of the Peisistratidae was at an end, the Athenians put up a bronze lioness in memory of the woman, which they say Callias dedicated and Calamis made.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a specific instance where the Athenian state or elite citizens commissioned a public monument to honor a woman, Leaena, for her resistance to tyranny. This indicates that women, though generally excluded from political life, could be granted high-status public recognition for extraordinary civic contributions.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: While public monuments were common, commissioning one specifically for a non-royal woman was a significant and somewhat rarer act of civic recognition.

extracted_rules.resource: Protection from execution during war
proof: [§159] Of the inhabitants the Thracians put to the sword not only the combatants but also the women and children. I have evidence to bring. All the Boeotian towns which the Thebans sacked were inhabited in my time, as the people escaped just before the capture; so if the foreigners had not exterminated the Mycalessians the survivors would have afterwards reoccupied the town.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Pausanias distinguishes between the 'civilized' Greek practice (where populations usually manage to survive or escape) and the 'barbarous' behavior of mercenaries/foreigners who systematically slaughtered women and children, indicating a context-dependent loss of protection for non-combatants.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule regarding the treatment of non-combatants during sackings concerns entire populations of towns.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to return to home city
proof: [§159] Oenobius performed a kind service for Thucydides the son of Olorus. He succeeded in getting a decree passed for the return of Thucydides to Athens, who was treacherously murdered as he was returning.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text mentions a specific legal mechanism (a decree passed by the assembly) used to restore a citizen's right to live in their home city after being exiled.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: Individual decrees for repatriation apply to specific people or families but are part of a broader legal framework for managing the status of exiles.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to hold high military command
proof: [§159] Quite one of the best men at Athens and distinguished for the fame of his ancestors he chanced to be heavily in debt. So he withdrew to the parish Paeania and lived there until the Athenians elected him to command a naval expedition. But he refused the office on the ground that before his debts were discharged he lacked the spirit to face his troops.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Phormio’s refusal, based on the social/moral weight of debt, implies a customary rule where a debtor lacks the necessary standing or 'spirit' to lead citizens in war until their financial obligations at home are met.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to elite citizens eligible for command who fall into financial distress, a specific subgroup of the citizen body.

extracted_rules.resource: Public payment of private debts
proof: [§159] So the Athenians, who were absolutely determined to have Phormio as their commander, paid all his creditors.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The Athenian state possessed the power to use public funds to clear the private debts of individuals they deemed essential for military service, granting a concrete material benefit to favored leaders.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This is a very specific occurrence where the state intervenes in the private finances of a single high-ranking individual.

extracted_rules.resource: Protection from homicide charges for ritual slaughter
proof: [§174] One of the priests they call the ox-slayer, who kills the ox and then, casting aside the axe here according to the ritual runs away. The others bring the axe to trial, as though they know not the man who did the deed.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The ritual of the Bouphonia involves a legal fiction where the priest is absolved of blood-guilt by the community putting the instrument (the axe) on trial instead of the human agent. This grants the specific group of sacrificial priests a unique legal protection from the consequences of killing a living being.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule applies specifically to a single designated priest (the 'ox-slayer') during a specific annual festival (the Diipolieia).

extracted_rules.resource: Right to own and govern island territories
proof: [§179] With Athens he nominally came to terms, but really imposed the severest penalties upon her, taking away the islands and putting an end to her maritime empire.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Pausanias describes the historical reality following the Battle of Chaeronea where the Macedonian king Philip II stripped the Athenian collective of their external land holdings (islands), which constitutes a loss of group property and resource access.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This affected the entire body of Athenian citizens who benefited from the revenues and land of the maritime empire.

extracted_rules.resource: Freedom from foreign military occupation
proof: [§179] A Macedonian garrison was set over the Athenians, and occupied first Munychia and afterwards Peiraeus also and the Long Walls.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Following the Lamian War, the Macedonian authorities imposed a garrison. This represents a loss of self-governance and freedom of movement within their own city for the citizens.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: The occupation affected the entire population of the city of Athens.

extracted_rules.resource: Retention of communal religious treasures
proof: [§179] Lachares took golden shields from the Acropolis, and stripped even the statue of Athena of its removable ornament
extracted_rules.reasoning: The tyrant Lachares seized communal/religious assets (the gold of the Parthenos) for his own use, depriving the citizen body of their managed religious and financial reserves.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: While the theft was by one man, it represented a loss for the entire community of Athens whose collective identity and gold reserves were tied to these religious objects.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to maintain maritime empire
proof: [§182] with Athens he nominally came to terms, but really imposed the severest penalties upon her, taking away the islands and putting an end to her maritime empire.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Following the battle of Chaeronea, Philip of Macedon imposed legal and political penalties on the Athenian state, specifically stripping them of their overseas territories and their right to maintain a naval hegemony.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule applies to the entire collective body of Athenian citizens and their state functions.

extracted_rules.resource: Freedom from foreign military garrisons
proof: [§184] A Macedonian garrison was set over the Athenians, and occupied first Munychia and afterwards Peiraeus also and the Long Walls.

[§187] Demetrius the son of Antigonus did not restore the Peiraeus to them immediately after the flight of Lachares, but subsequently overcame them and brought a garrison even into the upper city, fortifying the place called the Museum.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes several instances where the Macedonian authorities placed troops within the city and its ports to control the local population, effectively removing their right to self-governed urban space.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This affects all residents of the city of Athens and its port.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to public monuments and cultic dedications
proof: [§190] and when he fell fighting, the Athenians did him great honor, dedicating his shield to Zeus of Freedom and in scribing on it the name of Leocritus and his exploit.
[§191] Olympiodorus has not only honors at Athens, both on the Acropolis and in the town hall but also a portrait at Eleusis.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The Athenian state maintained a practice of granting concrete material and religious honors (statues, inscriptions, dedications in sacred spaces) to citizens who performed exceptional military or political services.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: While any citizen could theoretically earn this, it was reserved for a very small number of elite achievers or falling heroes.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to perform animal sacrifice
proof: [§193] Before the entrance is an altar of Zeus the Most High, on which they never sacrifice a living creature but offer cakes, not being wont to use any wine either.
extracted_rules.reasoning: At the specific altar of Zeus the Most High in the Erechtheum, there is a customary religious prohibition against animal sacrifice and libations of wine, restricting the types of offerings allowed.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule is highly specific to a single location (the Erechtheum) but applies to anyone attempting to worship there.

extracted_rules.resource: Exemption from active combat service
proof: [§189] He led them against the Macedonians, both the old men and the youths, and trusted for military success more to enthusiasm than to strength.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The mention of 'old men' and 'youths' being led to battle by Olympiodorus is framed as an exceptional circumstance contrasting with normal military practice, where those outside the prime age of service would typically be exempt from frontline duty.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: The mobilization of those usually exempt from service (the very old and very young) was a significant but broad exception to standard rules of age-based military drafts.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to reside on the Acropolis for sacred duties
proof: [§199] Two maidens dwell not far from the temple of Athena Polias, called by the Athenians Bearers of the Sacred Offerings. For a time they live with the goddess, but when the festival comes round they perform at night the following rites... These maidens they henceforth let go free, and take up to the Acropolis others in their place.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a specific, ongoing religious institution in Athens (the Arrephoroi) where a specific demographic (young maidens) is granted the exclusive right and duty to reside on the Acropolis and perform secret nocturnal rites for Athena.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule applies to a very small, rotating group of only two girls at a time, making it highly specific.

extracted_rules.resource: Knowledge of sacred objects
proof: [§199] Having placed on their heads what the priestess of Athena gives them to carry—neither she who gives nor they who carry have any knowledge what it is—the maidens descend by the natural underground passage that goes across the adjacent precincts.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Even while performing high-level religious duties, the participants (the maidens and the priestess) are systematically denied knowledge of the contents of the sacred offerings, establishing a rule of ritual secrecy based on role.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies only to the specific individuals involved in the Arrephoria rites.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to evacuate during siege
proof: [§202] For they too were caught by the flames when the Athenians had gone on board their ships and the King captured the city emptied of its able-bodied inhabitants.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Pausanias notes that the city was emptied of its 'able-bodied' population before the Persian capture, implying a systematic evacuation where those capable of fighting or fleeing were prioritized or mobilized to the fleet.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This concerns a large segment of the population (all able-bodied men or citizens).

extracted_rules.resource: Right to offer sacrifices
proof: [§213] There are images of Pluto, Hermes, and Earth, by which sacrifice those who have received an acquittal on the Hill of Ares; sacrifices are also offered on other occasions by both citizens and aliens.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Pausanias notes that both citizens and non-citizen residents (aliens) are permitted to offer sacrifices at the sanctuary of the August goddesses, indicating that religious participation in this civic space was not restricted to the citizen body.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule applies broadly to all non-citizens residing in or visiting Athens.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to trial for accidental killing
proof: [§215] One of the other courts that deal with bloodshed is called “At Palladium,” into which are brought cases of involuntary homicide.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The existence of the Palladium court specifically for involuntary homicide shows that Athenian law provided a formal legal mechanism for those who killed without intent, protecting them from immediate extrajudicial retribution.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies specifically to a subset of legal defendants involved in accidental deaths.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to plead justifiable homicide
proof: [§217] At Delphinium are tried those who claim that they have committed justifiable homicide, the plea put forward by Theseus when he was acquitted, after having killed Pallas, who had risen in revolt against him, and his sons.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The Delphinium court allows defendants to argue that a killing was legally justified (e.g., in response to revolt), providing a legal pathway to acquittal rather than automatic punishment for bloodshed.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to defendants in specific types of homicide cases.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to legal defense from a ship
proof: [§218] Near the sea at the Peiraeus is Phreattys. Here it is that men in exile, when a further charge has been brought against them in their absence, make their defense on a ship while the judges listen on land.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The court at Phreattys provides a specific legal mechanism for exiles to defend themselves against new charges without violating their banishment from the land, preserving their right to be heard.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This is a very specific procedural rule for a small subset of the population (exiles facing additional charges).

extracted_rules.resource: Protection from summary destruction of property
proof: [§217] The Court in the Prytaneum, as it is called, where they try iron and all similar inanimate things, had its origin, I believe, in the following incident... the axe was forthwith tried and acquitted, and the trial has been repeated year by year down to the present.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Applying legal trials to inanimate objects that caused death implies that even property was subject to a formal process at the Prytaneum before being disposed of or 'punished'.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This concerns a very specific and unusual Athenian legal ritual involving objects.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to public funeral and inscribed monument
proof: [§223] There is also a monument for all the Athenians whose fate it has been to fall in battle, whether at sea or on land, except such of them as fought at Marathon. These, for their valor, have their graves on the field of battle, but the others lie along the road to the Academy, and on their graves stand slabs bearing the name and parish of each.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Pausanias describes the Kerameikos as the site where the Athenian state systematically provided public burials and monuments for citizens who died in war, categorized by their name and deme (parish).
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule applies to all citizens who die in military service, a significant but specific portion of the population.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to public funeral and commemorative inscription
proof: [§226] It was surely a just decree even for a democracy when the Athenians actually allowed slaves a public funeral, and to have their names inscribed on a slab, which declares that in the war they proved good men and true to their masters.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text explicitly mentions a specific Athenian decree that granted slaves the right to a public funeral and an inscribed monument if they fought and died for the state, which was an exception to their usual status.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies only to a subgroup of slaves—those who fought in war—rather than the slave population as a whole.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to have name inscribed on war monument
proof: [§219] The names of the generals are inscribed with the exception of Nicias... This is the reason why Nicias was passed over, and my account is identical with that of Philistus, who says that while Demosthenes made a truce for the others and excluded himself, attempting to commit suicide when taken prisoner, Nicias voluntarily submitted to the surrender. For this reason Nicias had not his name inscribed on the slab, being condemned as a voluntary prisoner and an unworthy soldier.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text records a rule where soldiers or generals who surrendered voluntarily (rather than fighting to the death or attempting suicide) were excluded from the public honor of having their names inscribed on state war monuments.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule targets a very specific set of individuals based on their conduct during a military defeat.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to belong to a tribal administrative system
proof: [§225] Again there are monuments to Athenians: to Cleisthenes, who invented the system of the tribes at present existing
extracted_rules.reasoning: The invention of the tribal system by Cleisthenes established the fundamental organizational structure for Athenian citizenship and political participation.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This system encompassing all Athenian citizens is a broad societal foundation.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to name inscription on state monuments
proof: [§231] [12] The names of the generals are inscribed with the exception of Nicias, and among the private soldiers are included the Plataeans along with the Athenians. This is the reason why Nicias was passed over, and my account is identical with that of Philistus, who says that while Demosthenes made a truce for the others and excluded himself, attempting to commit suicide when taken prisoner, Nicias voluntarily submitted to the surrender. For this reason Nicias had not his name inscribed on the slab, being condemned as a voluntary prisoner and an unworthy soldier.
extracted_rules.reasoning: In Athenian customary law, a general who surrendered voluntarily rather than dying or attempting suicide in defeat was considered unworthy of the public funerary honor of having their name inscribed on the state war memorial.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies specifically to high-ranking military commanders (generals) who find themselves in a specific failure state (surrender).

extracted_rules.resource: Right to burial in the state cemetery
proof: [§231] [12] Among the private soldiers are included the Plataeans along with the Athenians.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text confirms that Plataeans, though technically from a different city-state, were granted the right to be buried and memorialized alongside Athenian citizens in the public cemetery because of their status as loyal allies.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to an entire sub-group of foreigners (Plataeans) serving in the military.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to dedicate public altars
proof: [§236] The altar within the city called the altar of Anteros (Love Avenged) they say was dedicated by resident aliens, because the Athenian Meles, spurning the love of Timagoras, a resident alien, bade him ascend to the highest point of the rock and cast himself down.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a specific altar dedicated collectively by metics (resident aliens), indicating they had the legal/customary right to establish and participate in cultic life within the city.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to the generic class of resident aliens (metics) in Athens.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to reciprocal love/marriage with citizens
proof: [§236] ...the Athenian Meles, spurning the love of Timagoras, a resident alien, bade him ascend to the highest point of the rock and cast himself down.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The story of Meles and Timagoras reflects a social stratification where the love of a resident alien for a citizen is treated as an overstep or a cause for humiliation, leading to the establishment of a cult specifically for 'Love Avenged.'
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: While a narrative, it reflects the social barriers between the group of 'citizens' and 'resident aliens'.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to participate in Panhellenic sacred transmissions
proof: [§241] Hither they say are sent the first-fruits of the Hyperboreans, and the Hyperboreans are said to hand them over to the Arimaspi, the Arimaspi to the Issedones, from these the Scythians bring them to Sinope, thence they are carried by Greeks to Prasiae, and the Athenians take them to Delos.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text outlines a formal customary chain of transmission for sacred offerings where specific non-Greek and Greek groups have the recognized right and obligation to handle and pass on sacred materials.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to specific named ethnic groups along a trade/religious route.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to serve in the military
proof: [§248] On the plain is the grave of the Athenians, and upon it are slabs giving the names of the killed according to their tribes; and there is another grave for the Boeotian Plataeans and for the slaves, for slaves fought then for the first time by the side of their masters.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text explicitly mentions that slaves participated in the Battle of Marathon alongside their masters, marking a shift where they were granted the right/obligation to fight in a major civic military engagement.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: While applying to the slave population, the text notes this was the 'first time', implying it was a specific historical exception rather than a universal standing right for all slaves at all times.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to formal burial and monument
proof: [§248] On the plain is the grave of the Athenians, and upon it are slabs giving the names of the killed according to their tribes; and there is another grave for the Boeotian Plataeans and for the slaves, for slaves fought then for the first time by the side of their masters.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The passage describes a specific grave and monument designated for slaves who fell in battle, indicating they were granted the resource of formal state-recognized burial and commemoration usually reserved for citizens or allies.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This is highly specific to those slaves who participated in the Battle of Marathon and died there.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to be buried after death
proof: [§253] Although the Athenians assert that they buried the Persians, because in any case the divine law applies that a corpse should be laid under the earth, yet I could find no grave.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text cites a 'divine law' that requires all corpses (including defeated foreign enemies like the Persians) to be buried, granting even non-citizens the right to basic post-mortem bodily integrity.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule is described as applying to 'in every case', meaning it encompasses all humans regardless of status.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to a trial before punishment
proof: [§252] Miltiades, the son of Cimon, although his end came later, after he had failed to take Paros and for this reason had been brought to trial by the Athenians.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The mention of Miltiades being 'brought to trial' for a military failure indicates that even high-ranking individuals were subject to the legal process and had the right/obligation to defend their actions in court.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: Legal trials were a standard feature for citizens in the Athenian democracy.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to consult oracle without prior sacrifice
proof: [§270] One who has come to consult Amphiaraus is wont first to purify himself. The mode of purification is to sacrifice to the god, and they sacrifice not only to him but also to all those whose names are on the altar. And when all these things have been first done, they sacrifice a ram, and, spreading the skin under them, go to sleep and await enlightenment in a dream.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Pausanias describes the mandatory ritual steps required for any person seeking an oracular dream at the sanctuary of Amphiaraus. The necessity of multiple sacrifices and a specific purification process constitutes a conditional barrier to accessing the divine resource.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule applies to a specific subset of people (those seeking oracular guidance) at a specific geographic sanctuary.

extracted_rules.resource: Retention of gold and silver coins
proof: [§269] But when a man has been cured of a disease through a response the custom is to throw silver and coined gold into the spring, for by this way they say that Amphiaraus rose up after he had become a god.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a customary requirement for those successfully cured at the shrine to surrender material wealth (gold and silver) into a sacred spring as a ritual obligation.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to inherit the throne

extracted_rules.reasoning: The text specifies an annual contest in honor of Leonidas and Pausanias where competition is strictly limited to the citizen group (Spartans), excluding all others.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: Applies to any non-Spartan seeking to participate in these specific games.

extracted_rules.resource: Freedom from mandatory ritual expenditure
proof: [§707] A mother is wont to sacrifice to the goddess when a daughter is married.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a customary obligation for mothers to perform a sacrifice (involving cost/resource) upon a daughter's marriage.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: Applies to all mothers of daughters in the Lacedaemonian society.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to inherit the throne
proof: [§710] Having no male issue, he left the kingdom to Lacedaemon... Lacedaemon was wedded to Sparta, a daughter of Eurotas.
extracted_rules.reasoning: When a king (Eurotas) has no male issue, the throne does not pass to his daughter (Sparta) but to a male (Lacedaemon) who marries her, indicating women were excluded from direct succession.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to royal women, a highly specific subgroup of the population.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to hold simultaneous kingship
proof: [§713] In Lacedaemon, as the sons of Aristodemus were twins, there arose two royal houses; for they say that the Pythian priestess approved.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text explains the origin of the uniquely Spartan dual-kingship (diarchy) based on the specific biological status of the heirs being twins, which created two parallel royal lines.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This is an extremely specific rule applying only to the founding twins of the Spartan royal houses.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to hold kingship over commoners
proof: [§716] ...taking into account that the family of Theras went back to Cadmus himself, while they were only descendants of Membliarus, who was a man of the people whom Cadmus left in the island to be the leader of the settlers.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a voluntary surrender of kingship based on the superiority of lineage. Those with divine/royal ancestry (Cadmus) are viewed as having a greater right to rule than those descended from a 'man of the people'.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This reflects a general principle used to settle claims between different groups in the narrative.

extracted_rules.resource: Freedom from enslavement
proof: [§718] In his reign the Lacedaemonians took by force of arms Aegys, a city of the Perioeci, and sold the inhabitants into slavery, suspecting them of Arcadian sympathies.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The Perioeci (dwellers around), while having a distinct status from Spartan citizens, were vulnerable to mass enslavement by the Spartan state if suspected of political disloyalty.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to entire urban populations within the Spartan territory.

extracted_rules.resource: Freedom of movement and residence
proof: [§718] When Echestratus, son of Agis, was king at Sparta, the Lacedaemonians removed all the Cynurians of military age, alleging as a reason that freebooters... were harrying Argolis.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The Spartan state exercised the power to forcibly remove specific age-cohorts of subject populations (the Cynurians) based on security concerns.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This affects a broad demographic (military age) of a specific geographic/ethnic group.

extracted_rules.resource: Freedom from enslavement
proof: [§723] In his reign the Lacedaemonians took by force of arms Aegys, a city of the Perioeci, and sold the inhabitants into slavery, suspecting them of Arcadian sympathies.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text demonstrates that the Perioeci, while usually having a distinct status, could be collectively enslaved and have their property/freedom revoked by the Spartan state upon suspicion of political disloyalty.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: The rule applies to a specific sub-class of subjects (the Dwellers-around) in a regional context.

extracted_rules.resource: Freedom from enslavement
proof: [§726] When the war against Messene had been fought to a finish, and Messenia was enslaved to the Lacedaemonians...
[§726] The remnant of them left behind in the land became the slaves of the Lacedaemonians, with the exception of those in the towns on the coast.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes the systematic reduction of a conquered population (Messenians) to the status of state slaves, specifically those who remained on the land as agricultural laborers (Helots).
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to an entire ethnic population within a conquered territory.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to hold multiple wives
proof: [§735] Anaxandrides the son of Leon was the only Lacedaemonian to possess at one and the same time two wives and two households. For his first consort, though an excellent wife, had the misfortune to he barren. When the ephors bade him pot her away he firmly refused to do so, but made this concession to them, that he would take another wife in addition to her.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The fact that Anaxandrides was the 'only' one to have two wives and that the ephors had to grant a specific 'concession' indicates a standard social and legal rule of monogamy for Spartan men.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This describes a general matrimonial law for the male citizen body.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to inherit the throne
proof: [§736] And when Anaxandrides died, the Lacedaemonians, believing Dorieus to be both of a sounder judgment than Cleomenes and a better soldier, much against their will rejected him as their king, and obeyed the laws by giving the throne to the elder claimant Cleomenes.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text explicitly states that the law required the throne to go to the elder son, even if the younger son was considered more competent or better suited by the people.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule specifically governs the succession of the royal houses in Sparta.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to retain a childless wife
proof: [§735] For his first consort, though an excellent wife, had the misfortune to he barren. When the ephors bade him pot her away he firmly refused to do so...
extracted_rules.reasoning: The intervention of the ephors to 'bid' the king to put away his wife due to her barrenness indicates a customary or legal pressure (and potential rule) that marriage was primarily for producing heirs, and childless wives should be replaced.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: Applies to married men, though the enforcement likely varied by status and need for heirs.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to inherit the throne
proof: [§744] Cleomenes had no male issue, and the kingdom devolved on Leonidas, son of Anaxandrides and full brother of Dorieus.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text explicitly links the transition of the kingship to a different male relative (Leonidas) to the fact that the previous king had no 'male issue,' implying women were excluded from the line of succession.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: The rule concerns a very small number of individuals within the royal lineage of Sparta.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to exercise kingly power directly
proof: [§746] For while guardian of Pleistarchus, the son of Leonidas, who was a child when his father died, he led the Lacedaemonians to Plataea, and afterwards with their fleet to the Hellespont.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text indicates that because the heir (Pleistarchus) was a child, a guardian (Pausanias) had to lead the army and exercise the functions of the king, showing that age was a barrier to the exercise of royal duties.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies specifically to royal heirs who are minors.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to inherit the throne
proof: [§741] Leotychides seized upon the remark that Ariston in his ignorance blurted out when Demaratus was born, denying that he was his child. [§742] So Demaratus was deposed, not rightfully, but because Cleomenes hated him.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The removal of King Demaratus was predicated on the claim that he was not the biological child of the previous king, showing that being recognized as a legitimate biological descendant was a requirement for the resource of kingship.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule applies only to candidates for the throne during succession disputes.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to retain personal property
proof: [§747] When Mardonius fell in the battle of Plataea, and the foreigners were destroyed, Pausanias sent the lady back to Cos, and she took with her the apparel that the Persian had procured for her as well as the rest of her belongings.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The action of the commander Pausanias in returning a captive woman with her personal goods is noted as a rule of decent treatment (which he 'cannot praise too highly'), indicating a customary rule or expectation that high-born captives might retain their belongings.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to a specific subset of high-status war captives.

extracted_rules.resource: Protection from execution in sacred spaces
proof: [§738] Near the battlefield was a grove sacred to Argus, son of Niobe, and on being routed some five thousand of the Argives took refuge therein. ... The Argives assert that the manner of his end was a punishment for his treatment of the suppliants of Argus.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes the burning of the grove as an act of 'mad excitement' and a violation that invited divine punishment, proving there was a societal rule against killing those who took refuge in a sacred grove (suppliants).
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule applies to any person (soldiers or locals) seeking sanctuary during warfare.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to trial by specific high court
proof: [§750] The court that sat to try a Lacedaemonian king consisted of the senate, “old men” as they were called, twenty eight in number, the members of the ephorate, and in addition the king of the other house.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text defines a specific legal composition for the trial of a king, ensuring they are judged by their peers (the other king) and the highest administrative bodies (senate and ephors) rather than a general assembly or standard court.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule applies only to the two sitting kings of Sparta, a very small group.

extracted_rules.resource: Protection from extradition
proof: [§754] Now this sanctuary had been respected from early days by all the Peloponnesians, and afforded peculiar safety to its suppliants, as the Lacedaemonians showed in the case of Pausanias and of Leotychides before him, and the Argives in the case of Chrysis; they never wanted even to ask for these refugees, who were sitting as suppliants in the sanctuary, to be given up.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a customary international rule where individuals facing trial or punishment (refugees/suppliants) are protected from being forcefully removed or extradited once they enter a specific sanctuary.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: While any person could technically become a suppliant, it specifically concerns those fleeing legal or political persecution.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to a guardian
proof: [§755] When Pausanias fled, his sons Agesipolis and Cleombrotus were still quite boys, and Aristodemus, their nearest relative, was their guardian.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text indicates that when royal children are left without a father, a 'nearest relative' is appointed as a guardian to manage their interests, showing a rule for the legal protection of minors.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to all children in the society who lose their primary legal protector.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to invoke ancestral truces
proof: [§756] When he led his army from the territory of Tegea into that of Argos, the Argives sent a herald to make for them with Agesipolis a certain ancestral truce, which from ancient times had been an established custom between Dorians and Dorians.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text identifies a specific customary law where groups of Dorian origin have the right to request a specific type of 'ancestral truce' during conflict based on shared lineage.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to a specific ethnic/linguistic subgroup within the Greek world.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to inherit the throne
proof: [§760] So the senators acted as arbitrators, and decided that the dignity was the inheritance of Areus the son of Acrotatus, and not of Cleonymus.

extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule only affects the tiny pool of legitimate heirs to the two Spartan royal houses.

extracted_rules.group: Male descendants of the Agiadae house
extracted_rules.resource: Right to inherit the throne
proof: [§765] And as the only male representative of the house of Eurysthenes was Leonidas the son of Cleonymus, by this time a very old man, the Lacedaemonians gave him the throne.

extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule applies only to a handful of individuals belonging to the two royal Spartan lineages.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to remain in the territory
proof: [§766] But as it was, when the citizens sentenced him to exile, he went to Arcadia, whence not many years later he was recalled by the Lacedaemonians, who made him king again.

extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: While applying to kings, it reflects a constitutional mechanism involving the citizen body and specific high-ranking individuals.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to inherit the throne
proof: [§775] Ariston... declared that because of the number of months the child was not his. [§776] Demaratus, a king of good repute at Sparta... became a private citizen through the thoughtlessness of Ariston and the hatred of Cleomenes.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Demaratus was removed from the kingship and reduced to a 'private citizen' specifically because his father publicly questioned his biological legitimacy based on the timing of his birth. This shows that the resource of the throne required recognized legitimate lineage.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule specifically targets royal heirs whose biological parentage is disputed.

extracted_rules.resource: Protection from arrest through sanctuary
proof: [§778] Or, being brought to trial in Lacedaemon he voluntarily went into exile to Tegea, where he sought sanctuary as a suppliant of Athena Alea.

extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: Religious sanctuary was a general rule available to various individuals facing prosecution or threat.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to inherit the throne
proof: [§778] Zeuxidamus, the son of Leotychides, died of disease while Leotychides was still alive and before he retired into exile so his son Archidamus succeeded to the throne after the departure of Leotychides for Tegea.

extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: Applies only to royal heirs in specific genealogical circumstances.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to inherit the throne
proof: [§781] Archidamus left sons when he died, of whom Agis was the elder and inherited the throne instead of Agesilaus.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text explicitly states that the elder son inherited the throne over the younger brother, identifying seniority among male heirs as the rule for royal succession in Sparta.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule applies only to the very narrow group of royal heirs (the Eurypontid and Agiad houses).

extracted_rules.resource: Right to compete in Olympic equestrian events
proof: [§781] Archidamus had also a daughter, whose name was Cynisca; she was exceedingly ambitious to succeed at the Olympic games, and was the first woman to breed horses and the first to win an Olympic victory. After Cynisca other women, especially women of Lacedaemon, have won Olympic victories, but none of them was more distinguished for their victories than she.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Pausanias indicates that women, specifically starting with Cynisca, were permitted to breed horses and win Olympic victories, showing a customary opening of this specific resource to women.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: While any woman could theoretically breed horses, the wealth required restricted this to the elite.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to sacrifice and compete at Olympia
proof: [§783] In the reign of Agis the son of Archidamus the Lacedaemonians had several grievances against the people of Elis, being especially exasperated because they were debarred from the Olympic games and the sanctuary at Olympia.
[§785] So Thrasydaeus and the Eleans, reduced to dire extremities, agreed to [...] allow the Lacedaemonians to sacrifice to the god and to compete in the games at Olympia.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text documents a period where an entire ethnic/political group (Spartans) were legally excluded from a pan-Hellenic sanctuary by the controlling state (Elis), and the subsequent lifting of that ban.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule applied to the entire population of the Spartan state.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to inherit the throne
proof: [§788] After the death of Agis, Agesilaus tried to keep Leotychides from the throne, recalling to the minds of the Lacedaemonians what Agis once said about Leotychides.
[§790] Leotychides on this occasion said that these words pointed to Agesilaus, who was lame in one of his feet, while Agesilaus interpreted them as alluding to the illegitimacy of Leotychides.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The controversy over Leotychides' succession demonstrates that being perceived as a bastard (not the biological son of the king) served as a legal and customary bar to inheriting political power.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies specifically to claimants of the royal lineage.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to hold the kingship
proof: [§789] Never let thy sound limbs give birth to a kingdom that lame is. Too long then shalt thou lie in the clutches of desperate hardships; Turmoil of war shall arise, o'erwhelming men in its billows.
[§790] Leotychides on this occasion said that these words pointed to Agesilaus, who was lame in one of his feet
extracted_rules.reasoning: The oracle and the subsequent debate show that physical 'lameness' or disability was considered a disqualifying factor or a negative omen for the exercise of royal power in Sparta.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule specifically targets the physical state of royal candidates.

extracted_rules.resource: Protection from execution in sacred spaces
proof: [§804] When the Boeotians were put to flight, certain of them took refuge in the sanctuary of Athena surnamed Itonia. Agesilaus, although suffering from a wound received in the battle, did not sin against the suppliants.

By stating that the king did not 'sin' against them, Pausanias identifies a religious and customary rule that grants defeated soldiers protection from further violence once they have entered a sacred space.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: While applying to a specific context (war), it encompasses any soldier or person who can reach a sanctuary during conflict.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to seek arbitration before war
proof: [§802] The Athenians receiving early intimation of the Lacedaemonians' intentions, sent to Sparta begging them to submit their grievances to a court of arbitration instead of appealing to arms, but the Lacedaemonians dismissed the envoys in anger.

This indicates that Greek citizens of this period had a recognized customary right to request legal mediation to avoid military conflict, even if it could be refused.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule applies to the entire collective of citizens represented by their envoys in inter-polity relations.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to reside in wealthy provincial capitals
proof: [§796] Sardes, pre-eminent for its wealth and resources, had been assigned as a residence to the satrap of the coast region, just as Susa had been to the king himself.

extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule applies only to a very small number of high-ranking imperial officials.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to perform traditional native rites

extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to a specific regional sub-group of the Spartan military (the Amycleans) rather than the entire army.

extracted_rules.resource: Freedom from enslavement
proof: [§809] The Phocians were contemplating the cruel course of killing the Delphians of vigorous age, enslaving the women and children, and levelling the city itself to the ground; it was due to the intercession of Archidamus that they escaped this fate at the hands of the Phocians.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The passage describes the standard 'contemplated' fate for non-combatants in captured cities as enslavement, identifying women and children as the specific demographic group subject to this loss of freedom.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This describes a general practice in Greek warfare regarding captured populations.

extracted_rules.resource: Freedom from enslavement

He was buried at the public expense by the Achaeans, and his fate it was to lose his life on the field of battle. My statement is borne out by the inscription at Olympia: [...] Chilon of Patrae, son of Chilon, whom the Achaean folk Buried for my valour when I died in battle.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a specific instance of a citizen-athlete who, upon dying in battle, was granted a burial at the public expense (state-funded), which was a concrete material reward for military valor.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: While specifically for those who die in battle, it represents a standard honorary practice for a subset of the citizen body (soldiers/valorous men).

extracted_rules.resource: Right to external honorary statuary
proof: [§1696] By the side of Sodamas stands Archidamus, son of Agesilaus, king of the Lacedaemonians. Before this Archidamus no king, so far as I could learn, had his statue set up by the Lacedaemonians, at least outside the boundaries of the country.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Pausanias notes a customary restriction in Sparta where kings were historically denied the honor of having statues erected in their likeness outside of Spartan territory, a restriction of their symbolic political presence.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule applies only to the very small group of Spartan kings.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to local citizenship
proof: [§1696] Expelled from Cnossus by a political party he came to Himera, was given citizenship and won many honors besides. It was accordingly natural for him to be proclaimed at the games as a native of Himera.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text demonstrates that political exiles could be formally integrated into new poleis through the granting of citizenship, which allowed them to represent their new city in panhellenic competitions.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to individuals seeking refuge, a common enough occurrence in Greek political life to represent a known legal mechanism.

extracted_rules.resource: Freedom from enslavement
proof: [§1708] All the other grown men he massacred, selling the women and children as slaves in order to pay his mercenaries. This disaster befell Scotussa when Phrasicleides was archon at Athens.
extracted_rules.reasoning: During the capture of Scotussa, the customary rule of war at the time (as exercised by the tyrant Alexander) allowed for the systematic selling of non-combatant groups into slavery to raise funds.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule applied to entire populations of conquered cities, affecting thousands at once.

extracted_rules.resource: Protection from execution without trial
proof: [§1708] It happened that an assembly of the citizens was being held, and those who were assembled in the theater the tyrant surrounded with targeteers and archers, and shot them all down; all the other grown men he massacred.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes the total loss of protection for the lives of adult male citizens during a coup or conquest, where they are systematically killed rather than enslaved.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This pertains to the treatment of the entire adult male population of a city-state during war.

extracted_rules.resource: Protection from summary execution
proof: [§1710] [2] It happened that an assembly of the citizens was being held, and those who were assembled in the theater the tyrant surrounded with targeteers and archers, and shot them all down; all the other grown men he massacred, selling the women and children as slaves in order to pay his mercenaries.
extracted_rules.reasoning: In the context of the seizure of Scotussa, the text records that the tyrant Alexander of Pherae systematically executed all 'grown men' while sparing women and children for enslavement, indicating a total lack of bodily protection for adult males during such political/military subjugations.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: The rule targets a broad demographic (all adult males) of a specific city-state during a period of tyranny.

extracted_rules.resource: Freedom from enslavement
proof: [§1710] [2] all the other grown men he massacred, selling the women and children as slaves in order to pay his mercenaries.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes the standard practice of the time where, upon the capture of a city, women and children were treated as booty to be sold into slavery to generate revenue.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This describes the fate of a large portion of a city's population under typical conditions of conquest.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to inherit the throne
proof: [§1715] [7] Dareius, the bastard son of Artaxerxes, who with the support of the Persian common people put down Sogdius, the legitimate son of Artaxerxes, and ascended the throne in his stead
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text identifies Darius specifically as 'the bastard son' in contrast to the 'legitimate son' Sogdius. The fact that Darius had to 'put down' the legitimate heir with outside support implies that, by standard rule, the bastard son had less right to the throne than the legitimate one.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to royal aspirants, a very small and specific group within the elite.

extracted_rules.resource: Retention of personal wealth
proof: [§1718/1724] [6] Thereupon the umpires fined Theagenes a talent, to be sacred to the god, and a talent for the harm done to Euthymus, holding that it was merely to spite him that he entered for the boxing competition.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The Olympic umpires (Hellanodikai) had the civic power to levy heavy financial fines against athletes if they judged an entry to be made out of 'spite' rather than fair competition, leading to a loss of the athlete's material resources.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: Applies to high-level competitors at international games subjected to specific judicial oversight by umpires.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to financial compensation
proof: [§1718/1724] [6] For this reason they condemned him to pay an extra fine privately to Euthymus. [...] and as compensation to Euthymus did not enter for the boxing-match.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The rules of the Olympic games allowed for private financial compensation to be awarded to an athlete who suffered harm due to the improper conduct or 'spite' of an opponent.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: Applies to specific legal outcomes within the regulatory framework of the panhellenic games.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to public commemoration in gymnasiums
proof: [§1718/1721] [3] Paraballon, the father of Lastratidas, was first in the double foot-race, and he left to those coming after an object of ambition, by writing up in the gymnasium at Olympia the names of those who won Olympic victories.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes the custom of recording the names of victors in the public gymnasium, providing a concrete entitlement to public space for the recording of their achievements.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: Applies only to a narrow group of successful athletes in specific contests.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to attend the Olympic games
proof: [§1732] The sons too of the daughters of Diagoras practised boxing and won Olympic victories: in the men's class Eucles, son of Callianax and Callipateira, daughter of Diagoras; in the boys' class Peisirodus, whose mother dressed herself as a man and a trainer, and took her son herself to the Olympic games.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a mother (Callipateira) who had to disguise herself as a man and a trainer to attend the Olympic games with her son. This necessity for disguise implies a formal prohibition against women attending or participating in the games in their own capacity.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule applies to all women in Greek society, though it specifically concerns their access to a specific Panhellenic religious and sporting event.

extracted_rules.resource: Protection from summary execution
proof: [§1741] For when Isagoras the Athenian captured the Acropolis of the Athenians with a view to setting up a tyranny, Timasitheus took part in the affair, and, on being taken prisoner on the Acropolis, was put to death by the Athenians for his sin against them.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text records the execution of Timasitheus by the Athenians following his participation in an attempted coup (tyranny). This reflects the legal consequence of death for attempting to overthrow the democratic order.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: The rule specifically targets individuals participating in high-level political crimes like attempted tyranny.

extracted_rules.resource: Protection from capital punishment
proof: [§1736] Dorieus, he goes on to say, was at the time away from home in the interior of the Peloponnesus, and having been caught by some Lacedaemonians he was brought to Sparta, convicted of treachery by the Lacedaemonians and sentenced to death.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Dorieus, despite his high status as an Olympic victor, was sentenced to death by the Spartans upon being convicted of treachery (betraying an alliance). This demonstrates that the state enforced the death penalty for betrayal of civic/military interests.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to any citizen or ally accused of 'treachery', a specific legal charge.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to compete in the men's athletic class
proof: [§1741] Socrates of Pellene won the boys' race, and Amertes of Elis the wrestlers' match for boys at Olympia, besides beating all competitors in the men's wrestling match at Pytho.
[§1730] Acusilaus, who received a crown for boxing in the men's class; [...] These too succeeded in beating the boys at boxing at Olympia.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The athletic contests are strictly divided into 'boys' and 'men's' classes. This indicates that younger participants are restricted from the adult class (the men's class) based on their age status, until they reach a specific maturity level.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule applies to all young male athletes in the context of Panhellenic games.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to dedicate a commemorative statue
proof: [§1738] Alcaenetus too, son of Theantus, a Leprean, himself and his sons won Olympian victories. [...] All have their statues set up at Olympia.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Winning at the Olympic games granted the individual the specific legal/customary right to erect a permanent portrait statue in the sacred precinct (Altis), a significant material and civic privilege.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: The rule is specific to the small group of individuals who achieve victory in the games.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to retain victory prizes
proof: [§1748] At the Festival previous to this it is said that Cleomedes of Astypalaea killed Iccus of Epidaurus during a boxing-match. On being convicted by the umpires of foul play and being deprived of the prize he became mad through grief and returned to Astypalaea.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a formal legal/procedural mechanism where umpires (Hellanodikai) convict a competitor of 'foul play' (breaking competition rules) and strip them of their material prize and recognized status as victor.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule applies specifically to the class of competitive athletes at panhellenic games, a distinct but significant subset of the population.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to receive public sacrifices
proof: [§1748/1756] The response given by the Pythian priestess was, they say, as follows:—Last of heroes is Cleomedes of Astypalaea; Honor him with sacrifices as being no longer a mortal. So from this time have the Astypalaeans paid honors to Cleomedes as to a hero.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text outlines a civic transition where a specific person, recognized by the Delphic Oracle, is granted the legal/religious status of a 'hero,' which mandates the community provide concrete material resources (sacrifices) to him.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to a single individual elevated to a specific status, though it represents the broader category of hero-cult installation.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to dedicate commemorative statues
proof: [§1758] This Cleosthenes was the first of those who bred horses in Greece to dedicate his statue at Olympia. For the offering of Evagoras the Laconian consists of the chariot without a figure of Evagoras himself.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes the established custom and right of Olympic victors to set up statues of themselves and their equipment in the sacred precinct, a highly regulated form of public space usage and property display.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: The rule applies to any victor at the games, a prestigious but regularized group in Greek society.

extracted_rules.resource: Protection from summary execution
proof: [§1747] For when Isagoras the Athenian captured the Acropolis of the Athenians with a view to setting up a tyranny, Timasitheus took part in the affair, and, on being taken prisoner on the Acropolis, was put to death by the Athenians for his sin against them.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The passage documents that foreigners (a Delphian) who aided a potential tyrant were not afforded the protections of prisoners of war but were executed for crimes against the state ('sin against them').
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: Applies to foreign combatant captives during internal political strife.

extracted_rules.resource: Freedom to select protective gear
proof: [§1758] In course of time the helmet and greaves were taken from the armour of competitors by both the Eleans and the Greeks generally.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text records a change in competition rules where the authorities (the Eleans) removed specific pieces of armor (helmet and greaves) from the standardized equipment required for the race in armor, restricting the group's previous equipment options.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: Applies to a specific subset of specialized athletes.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to wear full defensive armor in competition
proof: [§1762] In course of time the helmet and greaves were taken from the armour of competitors by both the Eleans and the Greeks generally.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Pausanias describes a shift in the regulations for the 'race in full armor' at Olympia, where the state (the Eleans) and the wider Greek community removed specific defensive items (helmet and greaves) from the required equipment for competitors.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule applies specifically to a subset of athletes (those in the armed race) participating in the pan-Hellenic games, though it is a general rule for that category.

extracted_rules.reasoning: While technically 'lifeless things', the text describes them as a distinct category subject to legal prosecution (homicide) and the specific penalty of banishment, following the laws of Draco.
extracted_rules.resource: Protection from judicial banishment
proof: [§1768] So the Thasians dropped the statue to the bottom of the sea, adopting the principle of Draco, who, when he framed for the Athenians laws to deal with homicide, inflicted banishment even on lifeless things, should one of them fall and kill a man.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This focuses on a very narrow legal exception where an object is treated as an entity capable of being prosecuted.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to reside in home territory
proof: [§1768] ...the god instructed them to receive back the exiles. At this command they received them back...

extracted_rules.resource: Right to dedicate personal statues in sacred precincts
proof: [§1766] This Cleosthenes was the first of those who bred horses in Greece to dedicate his statue at Olympia.
[§1778] Aratus was also proclaimed at Olympia as victor in the chariot-race... The statue of Aratus was dedicated by the Corinthians.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The entire passage documents the established right (and custom) of victors and their families/cities to set up permanent material memorials (statues) in the Altis following an Olympic victory.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule applies to all winners of the Olympic games, which is a repeated and recognized social category.

extracted_rules.resource: Protection from execution following property damage
proof: [§1768] The citizens were enraged at what he had done, but one of them, a respected man of advanced years, bade them not to kill the lad, and ordered him to carry the image from his home back again to the market-place.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The intervention of the elder to prevent the killing of a nine-year-old boy for a cultic/property offense demonstrates an age-based mitigation of punishment, where restitution is favored over execution.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: The rule applies generally to minors who commit offenses that might otherwise provoke lethal force from the citizen body.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to retain private property
proof: [§1788] The statue of Astylus of Crotona is the work of Pythagoras; this athlete won three successive victories at Olympia, in the short race and in the double race. But because on the two latter occasions he proclaimed himself a Syracusan, in order to please Hiero the son of Deinomenes, the people of Crotona for this condemned his house to be a prison, and pulled down his statue set up by the temple of Lacinian Hera.
extracted_rules.reasoning: When an athlete (a citizen) identifies with a different city for political favor, his home city-state has the legal authority to seize and repurpose his private property (his house) as a public penalty, effectively stripping him of his property rights.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule applies specifically to high-profile citizens like Olympic victors whose public declarations affect the city's prestige.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to Olympic victory and crown
proof: [§1788] Similar in renown to Chionis was Hermogenes of Xanthus, a Lydian, who won the wild olive eight times at three Olympic festivals, and was surnamed Horse by the Greeks. Polites also you will consider a great marvel. This Polites was from Ceramus in Caria, and showed at Olympia every excellence in running.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text confirms that non-Greeks (Lydians, Carians) were permitted to compete in the Olympic games and receive the 'wild olive' (the crown), granting them equal access to the religious and civic prestige of the festival despite their foreign origin.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to all non-Greek participants who meet athletic qualifications.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to commemorative statues in sacred precincts
proof: [§1784] Callon the son of Harmodius and Hippomachus the son of Moschion, Elean by race, were victors in the boys' boxing-match. The statue of Callon was made by Daippus.
[§1788] The Eleans proclaimed Pheidolas the winner and allowed him to dedicate a statue of this mare.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Winning at Olympia grants the individual the specific legal/religious right to occupy space in the pan-Hellenic sanctuary with a permanent monument, a right controlled and granted by the Eleans (the sanctuary managers).
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: While winning is difficult, the rule itself applies generally to anyone who achieves the status of victor.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to be proclaimed victor for animal performance
proof: [§1797] The mare of the Corinthian Pheidolas was called, the Corinthians relate, Aura (breeze), and at the beginning of the race she chanced to throw her rider. But nevertheless she went on running properly... The Eleans proclaimed Pheidolas the winner and allowed him to dedicate a statue of this mare.
extracted_rules.reasoning: In equestrian events, the resource (the victory and the right to dedicate a statue) is granted to the owner of the resource (the horse) rather than the laborer (the rider), even if the rider is absent during the victory.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule applies specifically to the wealthy class capable of breeding and entering horses.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to compete in athletic contests
proof: [§1801] Pherias of Aegina, whose statue stands by the side of Aristophon the Athenian, at the seventy-eighth Festival was considered very young, and, being judged to be as yet unfit to wrestle, was debarred from the contest.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a specific instance where a competitor was legally barred from an Olympic contest by officials (the judges) specifically because his age/physical maturity was deemed insufficient, establishing a rule that access to resources like athletic competition was contingent on age-based fitness.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies specifically to young male athletes, a substantial but defined sub-group of the population attending festivals.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to compete in boys' category

extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule targets a specific age transition point (18 years old) for athletic competitors.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to dedicate a statue in the Altis

[§1805] Milo won six victories for wrestling at Olympia, one of them among the boys... It is further stated that Milo carried his own statue into the Altis.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Winning an Olympic contest granted the victor the specific legal and religious right to erect a permanent memorial (statue) within the sacred precinct (Altis), a resource denied to the general population.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to dedicate statues to commanders
proof: [§1812] The two statues of Pythes, the son of Andromachus, a native of Abdera, were made by Lysippus, and were dedicated by his soldiers.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text notes that soldiers under a commander (Pythes) had the social and legal capacity to dedicate statues within the sacred grounds, showing that professional military identity facilitated local access to honorific resources.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to enter the pentathlum competition
proof: [§1822] At the thirty-eighth Festival Eutelidas the Spartan won two victories among the boys, one for wrestling and one for the pentathlum, this being the first and last occasion when boys were allowed to enter for the pentathlum.

extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to all male minors (boys) participating in the Panhellenic games.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to request schedule adjustments from umpires

extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to a specific sub-set of the population (elite athletes) during active competition.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to public commemoration and statues
