extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: While any citizen could theoretically compete, only victors (a small group) received the concrete resource of a publicly-funded statue.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to dedicate a commemorative statue
proof: [§1830] [5] Quite close to the statue of Aristeides stands Menalces of Elis, Proclaimed victor at Olympia in the pentathlum... After him comes Brimias of Elis, victor in the men's boxing-match, Leonidas from Naxos in the Aegean, a statue dedicated by the Arcadians of Psophis, a statue of Asamon, victor in the men's boxing-match, and a statue of Nicander, who won two victories at Olympia in the double course.

extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: While any free Greek could theoretically compete, only the small subset of actual winners were granted the specific right to permanent statuary in the sanctuary.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to serve as a soothsayer
proof: [§1835] That he was the soothsayer of the clan of the Clytidae, Eperastus declares at the end of the inscription: Of the stock of the sacred-tongued Clytidae I boast to be, Their soothsayer, the scion of the god-like Melampodidae.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text identifies a specific lineage (the Clytidae) as having the exclusive or hereditary right to act as soothsayers, tracing their lineage back to mythological figures to justify this civic-religious role.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to a very specific, narrow lineage group (the Clytidae) within the broader Elean population.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to be proclaimed under a specific city's name
proof: [§1845] Sotades at the ninety-ninth Festival was victorious in the long race and proclaimed a Cretan, as in fact he was. But at the next Festival he made himself an Ephesian, being bribed to do so by the Ephesian people. For this act he was banished by the Cretans.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text highlights that an athlete's citizenship was a formal legal/customary tie. Changing it for money was seen as a violation of the bond with their home city, resulting in the concrete legal punishment of banishment.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule governs the behavior of all professional athletes representing city-states in the Panhellenic games.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to claim property through legal channels

extracted_rules.resource: Right to receive honorary statues from foreign cities

extracted_rules.resource: Freedom from enslavement
proof: [§1848] [3] The people of Lampsacus favoured the cause of the Persian king, or were suspected of doing so, and Alexander, boiling over with rage against them, threatened to treat them with utmost rigor. Enslave the women and children of the people of Lampsacus, raze the whole city even to the ground, and burn the sanctuaries of their gods.”

Anaximenes' request relies on this being the standard 'most rigorous' treatment expected from a conquering king.

extracted_rules.resource: Citizenship and residency
proof: [§1851] [6] Sotades at the ninety-ninth Festival was victorious in the long race and proclaimed a Cretan, as in fact he was. But at the next Festival he made himself an Ephesian, being bribed to do so by the Ephesian people. For this act he was banished by the Cretans.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to maintain sacred treasuries
proof: [§1853] There is in the Altis to the north of the Heraeum a terrace of conglomerate, and behind it stretches Mount Cronius. extracted_rules.resource: Right to dedicate spoils as architecture

extracted_rules.resource: Right to enter the inner sanctuary of Sosipolis
proof: [§1872] In the front part of the temple, for it is built in two parts, is an altar of Eileithyia and an entrance for the public; in the inner Part Sosipolis is worshipped, and no one may enter it except the woman who tends the god, and she must wrap her head and face in a white veil.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text explicitly divides the temple into a pulic area and an inner part where the general public is barred from entering, reserving access solely for the specific priestess.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to enter the Hippodameium

extracted_rules.resource: Right to enter the old sanctuary of Poseidon

extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: While the rule applies to 'nobody' (the public), it is specific to a single, highly significant geographical and religious location in Mantineia.
